event: Passion cycle: Nativity, Flagellation, Crucifixion, and Resurrection
symbols: four-panel altarpiece — the liturgical unfolding of salvation history from Incarnation to Resurrection
cross — the central axis of redemption, given prominence in the crucifixion panel
manger scene — the Incarnation, God entering human flesh in humility
flagellation column — the suffering and scourging of the innocent Lamb
risen Christ in radiant light — the victory over death and the inauguration of the new creation
gold gilding on frame — the heavenly glory that frames and sanctifies the earthly narrative of redemption
sequential panels — the theological narrative arc from birth through death to glorious resurrection
description: A large four-panel altarpiece (polyptych) displayed in a museum setting, with an ornate carved and gilded wooden frame surmounted by two dark panels with gold decorations. From left to right, the panels depict: the Adoration of the Magi with the Virgin and Child surrounded by richly dressed visitors beneath an architectural canopy; the Flagellation of Christ, showing Jesus being scourged at a column amid a crowd of tormentors in a palatial interior; the Crucifixion with Christ and two thieves on crosses against a dark sky, mourners gathered below; and the Resurrection, with the radiant rise...
theological_description: The Herrenberger Altar by Jerg Ratgeb presents the complete arc of salvation history in four interconnected panels, functioning as a visual catechism of Christological doctrine. The leftmost panel, the Adoration of the Magi, announces the Incarnation — the Word made flesh (John 1:14) — and the recognition of Christ's kingship by the Gentile nations, fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy that nations would come to his light (Isaiah 60:3). The Flagellation panel depicts the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53, 'by his stripes we are healed,' showing Christ enduring the scourging that Pilate ordered (John 19:1). The Crucifixion at the center-right position occupies the theological climax: Christ lifted up between two criminals fulfills his own prophecy in John 12:32, 'And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.' The presence of mourners below, including Mary, corresponds to John 19:25-27. The final panel, the Resurrection, completes the kerygmatic proclamation: Christ is risen, death is defeated, and the guards who witness it fall as though dead (Matthew 28:2-4). Read together as a liturgical whole, the four panels narrate the Paschal Mystery — the passage from Incarnation through suffering and death to triumphant resurrection — that constitutes the heart of Christian faith and worship.

scripture_refs.reason: The Adoration of the Magi panel directly depicts the visit of the wise men to the infant Christ
The Flagellation panel shows Pilate's order to scourge Jesus before his crucifixion
The Crucifixion panel depicts Christ on the cross at Golgotha with mourners below
The Resurrection panel shows the risen Christ emerging from the tomb while guards fall back in terror
The Suffering Servant prophecy underlies both the Flagellation and Crucifixion panels — wounded for our transgressions
The Nativity/Adoration panel embodies the Word becoming flesh and dwelling among us
The four panels together narrate the core kerygma: Christ died, was buried, and rose on the third day according to the Scriptures
